ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for roasted duck rice; mapped to the closest parent class 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_03315671) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for roasted duck rice; A0FJP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of roasted duck rice typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of sliced roasted duck over steamed white rice with sauce drizzled on top.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Duck Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Duck Rice (F05100113). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck and rice composition data as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for roasted duck rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (jasmine/long-grain), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 22.79 × 3.5) / 100 = 57.5 for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 251 mg per 100 g; typical 350 g serving delivers approximately 879 mg sodium, representing 37–44% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2400 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce and soy drizzle, limit portion size to no more than 250 g, and avoid adding extra condiments to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 22.79 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.06 g dietary fibre per 100 g produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 57.5 per standard serving, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce the rice portion to half, add a side of non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption, and avoid sweet plum or hoisin sauces to limit total glycaemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Duck skin contributes saturated fat (estimated ~1.6 g per 100 g) and the dish provides approximately 879 mg sodium per standard serving, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease when consumed in excess.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should request duck without skin to reduce saturated fat intake, limit sauce to reduce sodium, and pair the dish with steamed vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and fibre profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low dietary fibre (1.06 g per 100 g) and saturated fat from duck skin may contribute to glycaemic-load-driven and lipid-mediated inflammatory pathways relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request duck without skin, choose brown rice where available, and add steamed leafy greens to introduce antioxidants and reduce the pro-inflammatory dietary load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Duck Rice (烧鸭饭, Siu Ngap Fan) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of sliced Cantonese-style roasted duck served over steamed white rice, typically accompanied by a soy-based braising sauce and sometimes garnished with cucumber slices. The duck is marinated with five-spice, soy sauce, and aromatics, then roasted until the skin is lacquered and crisp while the meat remains tender and juicy. The dish is a staple of Singapore's hawker culture and is closely related to the Cantonese roast-meat tradition. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 585 kcal, 25 g protein, 19 g fat, and 80 g carbohydrate, with sodium around 879 mg. The white rice base carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making the dish a high-glycaemic-load meal unsuitable for uncontrolled Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content is borderline for hypertension management and increases substantially with additional sauce. The dish is not halal due to the use of pork-derived products in some preparations, though halal-certified versions exist. It is dairy-free and contains soy and gluten allergens from the soy sauce used in the marinade and braising sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in roasted duck rice?
Can diabetics eat roasted duck rice?
Is roasted duck rice high in sodium?
Is duck rice suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100113) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central duck and rice composition data as a secondary approximation. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is an approved GI source.
notes: Saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for roasted duck (USDA SR Legacy #05140) and cooked white rice (USDA SR Legacy #20045) in approximate dish proportions (~30% duck, ~60% rice, ~10% sauce), as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid profiles, micronutrient breakdowns, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or available in a directly applicable approved source as of April 2026. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component per the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for roasted duck rice as a composite dish has been published in an approved source. Halal status is marked false as traditional Cantonese roast duck preparations may involve non-halal ingredients; halal-certified variants exist at specific hawker stalls.
